lessons.title: Introduction to SELECT
lessons.content:
The `SELECT` statement retrieves rows from a table.

```sql
SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name;
```

Use `SELECT *` to retrieve all columns, though naming columns explicitly is best practice — it avoids breaking your code if the table's columns change later, and makes queries self-documenting.

**Column aliases**: rename output columns using `AS`:
```sql
SELECT first_name AS name, salary AS pay FROM employees;
```

lessons.title: SELECT with expressions
lessons.content:
You can compute values directly in a `SELECT` list, not just retrieve raw columns:

```sql
SELECT first_name, salary, salary * 12 AS annual_salary
FROM employees;
```

This works with string concatenation, math, and function calls, all inside the same query.

lessons.title: The WHERE clause
lessons.content:
`WHERE` filters rows based on a condition, evaluated per row before results are returned.

```sql
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000;
```

Combine conditions with `AND` / `OR`:
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 80000;
```

lessons.title: IN, BETWEEN, LIKE
lessons.content:
**IN** — match against a list of values:
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department IN ('Sales', 'Marketing');
```

**BETWEEN** — match an inclusive range:
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 80000;
```

**LIKE** — pattern matching with `%` (any characters) and `_` (single character):
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE email LIKE '%@company.com';
```

title: Sorting & Limiting Results
description: Control the order and quantity of returned rows.

lessons.title: ORDER BY
lessons.content:
`ORDER BY` sorts results. Default is ascending (`ASC`); use `DESC` for descending.

```sql
SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
```

Sort by multiple columns — ties in the first column are broken by the second:
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;
```

lessons.title: LIMIT and OFFSET
lessons.content:
`LIMIT` restricts the number of rows returned — essential for pagination and top-N queries.

```sql
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 5;
```

Combine with `OFFSET` to skip rows (e.g., for page 2 of results):
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10;
```

Note: MySQL/Postgres use `LIMIT`/`OFFSET`; SQL Server uses `OFFSET ... FETCH NEXT` instead.

title: DISTINCT & Removing Duplicates
description: Retrieve unique values from a column or combination of columns.

lessons.title: Using DISTINCT
lessons.content:
`DISTINCT` removes duplicate rows from the result set.

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT department FROM employees;
```

With multiple columns, `DISTINCT` considers the *combination* unique — not each column independently:
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT department, job_title FROM employees;
```
This returns unique department+job_title pairs, not all unique departments and all unique job titles separately.

title: Working with NULL
description: Understand how NULL behaves differently from other values.

lessons.title: NULL is not a value
lessons.content:
`NULL` means "unknown" or "absent", not zero or empty string. This has surprising consequences:

```sql
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE bonus = NULL;  -- returns NOTHING, always
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE bonus IS NULL; -- correct way to check
```

Any arithmetic or comparison with `NULL` produces `NULL`, not `true`/`false`. Use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL`, never `= NULL`.

**COALESCE** substitutes a default when a value is `NULL`:
```sql
SELECT name, COALESCE(bonus, 0) AS bonus FROM employees;
```

lessons.title: Common string functions
lessons.content:
```sql
SELECT
  UPPER(name) AS upper_name,
  LOWER(name) AS lower_name,
  LENGTH(name) AS name_length,
  CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name,
  TRIM(name) AS trimmed,
  SUBSTRING(name, 1, 3) AS first_three_chars
FROM employees;
```

Note: function names vary slightly by dialect — MySQL uses `CONCAT()`, Postgres also supports the `||` operator (`first_name || ' ' || last_name`).

title: Numeric & Date Functions
description: Perform calculations and work with dates/times.

lessons.title: Numeric functions
lessons.content:
```sql
SELECT
  ROUND(salary, 2) AS rounded,
  CEIL(salary) AS rounded_up,
  FLOOR(salary) AS rounded_down,
  ABS(profit_loss) AS absolute_value
FROM financials;
```

lessons.title: Date functions
lessons.content:
```sql
SELECT
  hire_date,
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM hire_date) AS hire_year,
  CURRENT_DATE - hire_date AS days_employed
FROM employees;
```

Date function names differ significantly across dialects (`DATEDIFF` in MySQL vs interval arithmetic in Postgres) — this is a common interview gotcha worth knowing.

title: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
description: Modify data, not just read it.

lessons.title: Modifying data
lessons.content:
**INSERT** adds new rows:
```sql
INSERT INTO employees (name, salary) VALUES ('Alice', 75000);
```

**UPDATE** modifies existing rows — always use `WHERE`, or every row gets updated:
```sql
UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.1 WHERE department = 'Engineering';
```

**DELETE** removes rows — same warning applies:
```sql
DELETE FROM employees WHERE id = 42;
```

A `WHERE`-less `UPDATE` or `DELETE` is one of the most common real-world SQL mistakes — always double check before running one in production.
